page.title: Polycythaemia Vera / Myeloproliferative Neoplasms — RepurpOS | OpenSourceMedicine
page.description: Structured alterations and therapeutics for Polycythemia Vera: molecular targets, clinical biomarkers, phenotypes, and ranked drugs from Open Targets, HPO, ChEMBL, and DGIdb.
page.hero: Disease intelligence for Polycythemia Vera: 144 alterations and 277 ranked therapeutics from curated public databases.

alterations.name: APC
alterations.definition: APC regulator of Wnt signaling pathway

alterations.name: ASAP1
alterations.definition: ArfGAP with SH3 domain, ankyrin repeat and PH domain 1

alterations.name: BCR
alterations.definition: BCR activator of RhoGEF and GTPase

alterations.name: EZH2
alterations.definition: enhancer of zeste 2 polycomb repressive complex 2 subunit

disclaimer: Alterations and therapeutics are aggregated from public databases (Open Targets, HPO, ChEMBL, DGIdb, and others). Natural agents are candidates from OSMF narrative reviews on opensourcemed.info/chronic-disease.html — not approved treatments. This page is for research orientation only — not medical advice.

remission.spontaneous_remission_rate: PV/ET/MF: no spontaneous remission; progressive disorders with risk of transformation to MF (PV/ET) and blast phase (all); median survival PV >20 years, MF 5-7 years
remission.best_intervention_remission_rate: JAK2 inhibitor (ruxolitinib) for MF/PV: 35-42% spleen volume reduction, significant symptom improvement; ropeginterferon alfa-2b: superior complete haematological response vs hydroxyurea (PROUD-PV); allogeneic HSCT (MF): curative in 30-60% of transplant-eligible patients
remission.gap_size: Moderate — ruxolitinib is established but HSCT underused in eligible MF patients; ropeginterferon is superior in PV but access varies
remission.barrier_detail: Hydroxyurea remains the most common cytoreductive agent for PV/ET despite ropeginterferon demonstrating superior molecular response; HSCT for MF is underutilised in patients who could benefit; JAK2 mutation molecular monitoring not performed systematically
remission.last_soc_change: 2022 — ropeginterferon alfa-2b (Besremi) FDA approved for PV; 2019 fedratinib (JAK2/FLT3) for MF; pacritinib 2022 for thrombocytopenic MF; ruxolitinib 2011 for MF, 2014 for PV; hydroxyurea standard since 1960s
remission.outdated_treatments: Phlebotomy alone for all PV without cytoreductive therapy in high-risk patients (standard in low-risk but insufficient in high-risk for thrombosis prevention); chlorambucil/busulfan for PV cytoreduction (leukaemogenic; replaced by hydroxyurea or interferon); anagrelide in all ET patients without thrombosis risk stratification
remission.adjunctive_emerging: Aspirin (low-dose — reduces thrombosis risk in PV; established since ECLAP trial 2004, yet not universally prescribed); cardiovascular risk factor optimisation; hematocrit <45% maintenance (CYTO-PV trial — critical for thrombosis risk reduction)
remission.frontier_signal: Pelabresib (BET inhibitor + ruxolitinib for MF — Phase 3 MANIFEST-2 positive 2023); navitoclax (BCL2/BCL-XL inhibitor + ruxolitinib — Phase 3 TRANSFORM-1 positive 2023); luspatercept for MF-related anaemia; CAR-T targeting CD123 in blast phase MPNs; luspatercept in PV for iron management
remission.notes: Manually curated from landmark trials, registries, and guidelines (disease_db_100.json). Protected from automated overwrite.
